explanation.text: Inez writes the case name and then writes the group’s first plan in her notebook. These entries show how the girls keep a record of their investigation as they gather clues.
Ivy says the garbage can has a different smell, but when the girls sniff the sofa she says, “That’s the stink.” This shows that the sofa smells the same as Ivy’s pillow.

The pillow, slippers, and sofa seem unrelated until Inez notices that each is soft and cushy. This clue is important because it connects all the smelly objects and eventually points the girls toward Baxter.
Izzy tells the girls to “check out” the area because they are following the smell and looking for its source. In this context, the phrase means to search or investigate behind the bushes.

The girls investigate matching smells, connect the objects to Baxter, and follow the odor to rotting eggs under the bushes. Therefore, discovering where the bad smell comes from is the story’s main idea.

Izzy, Inez, and Ivy all have names beginning with I, and they investigate clues to solve the bad-smell mystery. This shows that “Private I’s” is a playful version of “private eyes,” meaning detectives.
Mary Jane discovers that the art scraps she left on the table have disappeared. Because this creates the mystery that drives the story, it is the most important detail for a summary.
Josh explains that Smokey used to hide stolen toys behind the large brown chair. This information helps Mary Jane solve the problem because she searches there and finds her missing art scraps.
Mary Jane grunts while slowly pulling the chair away from the wall. The words “heavy chair” explain why moving it requires enough effort to make her grunt.

The tissue paper and broken sticks came from Mary Jane’s project the night before, but the hair ribbons had been missing already. This shows that Smokey has collected objects over a longer period of time.
Mary Jane turns discarded ice-pop sticks into borders and tissue paper into the colored glass of a pretend stained-glass window. This inventive reuse of ordinary materials shows that she is creative.

Mary Jane says the painted ice-pop sticks form the borders and the colored tissue paper represents glass. These details show that her project is a decorative stained-glass-style window made from both materials.
The inventors want something that can go faster and haul heavier loads than people or animals could. This context shows that a vehicle is something used to move people and goods.
Cugnot’s boiler heated water to make steam, and pressure from that steam forced the front wheel to turn. Because steam supplied the power, the automobile could move without an animal pulling it.

After introducing “drawbacks,” the passage lists short battery range, scarce charging places, weak performance in mud, and slow speed. These limitations show that electric cars were not a perfect solution.

These paragraphs move from battery-powered cars to faster, longer-ranging gasoline cars and then to Ford’s less expensive Model T. This progression supports the main idea because it shows inventors improving cars in several ways.
Electric cars could travel only about 20 to 40 miles before their batteries died. Gas-powered cars carried extra fuel in tanks, which means they could continue for longer distances.

Paragraph 14 explains that early gas cars cost more than most people could afford. Paragraphs 15 through 17 show how Ford’s fast assembly-line process solved that problem by producing a much cheaper car.
The headings move from the need for faster travel to steam, battery, and gasoline power. This organization shows different stages in how automobiles changed over time.
